coredata.dc:title: Bevacizumab and folic acid dual-targeted gadolinium-carbon dots for fluorescence/magnetic resonance imaging of hepatocellular carcinoma

coredata.dc:description: In this study, single- and dual-targeted gadolinium-carbon dots (Gd-CDs) with Bevacizumab (BEV) and/or folic acid (FA) were developed as dual-modal fluorescence and magnetic resonance (FL/MR) imaging nanoprobes. Gd-CDs were prepared by a single-pot hydrothermal route using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and Gd-DO3A-butrol (Gadovist) as the starting materials. Mouse hepatocellular carcinoma (Hepa1-6) cell line was selected as cancer cells, while mouse fibroblast (L929) was used as a normal cell line. Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV exhibited the high relaxivities (r1) of 7.98, 7.83, 7.61, and 6.03 mM−1 s−1, respectively, being considerably more than the clinical Gd-DO3A-butrol contrast agent (r1 = 4.01 mM−1 s−1). Moreover, they exhibited strong fluorescence with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 49.94%, 61.22%, 82.5%, and 83.67%, respectively. MTT assays on cancer and normal cells showed that various synthesized Gd-CDs on both cell lines have low toxicity. Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) analysis revealed that Gd-CDs-FA-BEV uptake by Hepa1-6 cells was 5-fold as much as the non-targeted Gd-CDs after 24 h incubation. Functionalization of Gd-CDs with Bevacizumab and/or folic acid improves the diagnostic sensitivities and specificities, making them ideal contrast agents for MRI as well as ideal fluorophores for fluorescence imaging.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Gd-CDs. High-resolution TEM images of Gd-CDs (a) and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV (b). EDS analysis of Gd-CDs-FA-BEV (c). XRD pattern of Gd-CDs (d). FTIR spectra of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV (e).

Ultraviolet–Visible absorption spectra and the photographs of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV (inset from left to right, respectively under natural light (top) and UV light (down) (a). Fluorescence emission spectra of various Gd-CDs at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm (b). Fluorescence emission spectra of Gd-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (320–400 nm) (c).
T1-weighted MR images (a) and longitudinal relaxivity plots (b) of various Gd-CDs and Gd-DO3A-butrol at different Gd concentrations.
Viability of Hepa1-6 (a) and L929 (b) cells treated with various Gd-CDs at different concentrations (0 (control), 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1 mM Gd) for 24 h incubation; means ± SD (n = 3).

The fluorescence microscopic images of Hepa1-6 (a) and L929 (b) cells treated with Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV at 0.6 mM Gd concentration for 6 h. Images were captured under bright-field, UV light excitation and overlay of bright-field and fluorescence images.

T1-weighted MR images of Hepa1-6 and L929 cells treated with Gd-CDs-FA-BEV at different Gd concentrations (0 (control), 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 mM) for 4 h on 1.5 T MRI system (a). Signal intensity analysis for MR images of cells (b). Mean ± SD (n = 3). *: p-value < 0.05, **: p-value < 0.01. Quantitative assessment of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV uptake in Hepa1-6 cells after different time incubation (6, 12 and 24 h) by ICP-OES (c). Mean ± SD (n = 3). *: p-value < 0.05, **: p <-value 0.01, compared to non-targeted (Gd-CDs) group.
Schematic illustration of synthesis and functionalization of Gd-CDs-FA with Bevacizumab and its application as dual-modal FL/MR imaging nanoprobe.

head.ce:title: Bevacizumab and folic acid dual-targeted gadolinium-carbon dots for fluorescence/magnetic resonance imaging of hepatocellular carcinoma

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, single- and dual-targeted gadolinium-carbon dots (Gd-CDs) with Bevacizumab (BEV) and/or folic acid (FA) were developed as dual-modal fluorescence and magnetic resonance (FL/MR) imaging nanoprobes. Gd-CDs were prepared by a single-pot hydrothermal route using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and Gd-DO3A-butrol (Gadovist) as the starting materials. Mouse hepatocellular carcinoma (Hepa1-6) cell line was selected as cancer cells, while mouse fibroblast (L929) was used as a normal cell line. Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV exhibited the high relaxivities (r1) of 7.98, 7.83, 7.61, and 6.03 mM−1 s−1, respectively, being considerably more than the clinical Gd-DO3A-butrol contrast agent (r1 = 4.01 mM−1 s−1). Moreover, they exhibited strong fluorescence with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 49.94%, 61.22%, 82.5%, and 83.67%, respectively. MTT assays on cancer and normal cells showed that various synthesized Gd-CDs on both cell lines have low toxicity. Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) analysis revealed that Gd-CDs-FA-BEV uptake by Hepa1-6 cells was 5-fold as much as the non-targeted Gd-CDs after 24 h incubation. Functionalization of Gd-CDs with Bevacizumab and/or folic acid improves the diagnostic sensitivities and specificities, making them ideal contrast agents for MRI as well as ideal fluorophores for fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the sixth most prevalent malignant tumor and the third leading cause of cancer death [1,2]. The five-year patient survival rate of HCC is lower than 16%. However, its early-stage diagnosis with a robust screening is essential, increasing the survival rate, as high as 93% [3]. The main tools in the HCC diagnosis are various imaging techniques including Computed Tomography (CT), ultrasound, Magnetic Resonance (MR), and fluorescence [4]. However, conventional imaging techniques suffer from some cancer diagnosis limitations including poor ability in early-stage cancer detection, besides small metastasis and distinction between benign and malignant tumors [5–7].

Recent developments in molecular imaging techniques resolve some of these limitations [6]. These methods offer the advantages of diagnosing cancer in the early stages, monitoring biological processes at molecular and cellular levels, identifying tumor region extent, applying a specific therapeutic method, and assessing therapeutic molecular-specific effects [8]. Furthermore, non-invasive molecular imaging methods are more convenient than biopsy, directly providing anatomical and molecular information about tissues and organs [9]. Despite many advantages of each imaging modality, they suffer from intrinsic limitations, such as low sensitivity, specificity, and spatial resolution in cancer detection [9,10]. Therefore, combination of two or more imaging techniques can integrate their complementary advantages and provide accurate anatomical and physiological information for cancer diagnosis [10,11]. For instance, MRI is a powerful non-invasive imaging technique, providing no ionizing radiation, deep tissue penetration, high spatial resolution, abundant pulse sequences, multi-planar (coronal, sagittal, and transverse) imaging of the structures, and function of soft tissues. However, the sensitivity of this technique is relatively low [12–16]. Compared to MRI, fluorescence imaging (FI) is a low-cost and portable technique [11]; it has high sensitivity but limited depth of penetration and poor spatial resolution [15,17,18]. Therefore, the combination of MRI and fluorescence techniques offers the advantages of both techniques, including tissue penetration, as well as high sensitivity and resolution [15,19]. Dual-modal FL/MR imaging is a promising strategy for theranostic applications [20]. MRI and fluorescence imaging contrast agents (CAs) should have properties, including high quantum yield (QY), relaxivity, biocompatibility, and stability in biological systems [15].

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures with less than 10 nm size that show intrinsic photoluminescence property [21,22]. They are favorite materials for imaging utilization due to their specific features, including good photo and chemical stability, low photobleaching, excellent biocompatibility, high water solubility, high fluorescent intensity, low cost, facile protocols for their synthesis, and abundant raw materials in nature [23–27]. Moreover, CDs possess many functional groups on their surfaces; thus, they can undergo subsequent functionalization with polymeric, organic, inorganic, or biological species [28]. Collectively, CDs with unique chemical, electronic, and optical properties have high potential applications in biomedicine fields, such as biolabeling, biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery [22].

Among the metal-based MRI contrast agents, gadolinium (Gd) with seven unpaired electron spins, is used in the clinic as the most general T1 positive CA due to its unique magnetic properties and excellent contrast efficiency [12,29]. However, free Gd3+ ion is a high toxic ion. To suppress the toxicity, many Gd-based agents have been developed, which mainly consist of central Gd3+ ion complexes with chelating ligands [30,31]. Some Gadolinium chelates, such as gadopentetate dimeglumine (Gd-DTPA, Magnevist), gadobutrol (Gd-DO3A-butrol, Gadovist), and gadodiamide (Gd-DTPA-BMA, Omniscan), are commercially available [30]. However, recent studies have reported the possibility of Gd3+ ions release from these complexes and their accumulation in the body that may cause biological toxicity, leading to nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF), especially in patients with renal dysfunction [32–34]. One approach is doping Gd3+ ions into CDs without losing the prominent MR relaxivity and fluorescent intensity so that the release of Gd3+ ions can be curtailed [31,34].

Most nanoparticles are expected to accumulate at the tumor region using the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect as a well-known passive targeting mechanism. However, passive targeting suffers from a variety of vascularization and porosity of vessels in tumor types and low specific binding of nanoparticles to cancer cells as well [35]. Therefore, an active targeting strategy for most sensing, diagnostic, or molecular imaging applications is necessary [36]. This approach can be achieved by functionalizing nanomaterials with various targeting bioligands, such as antibodies, peptides, aptamers, and folic acid [35,37]. These ligands can conjugate to the overexpressed receptors exclusively on target malignant tumor cells [35]. Active targeting improves the accuracy of tumor cell detection by increasing the targeting specificity and accumulating nanoparticles in tumor regions [38].

In recent years, dual-targeting strategies have been applied as promising approaches for imaging and therapy [39]. With bispecific probes, this method improves the cellular uptake of nanoprobes via synergistic targeting of two receptors that overexpress on the surface of cancer cells, compared to single-targeted or non-targeted nanoparticles [39,40]. These nanoprobes interact with two different receptors/proteins on a cancer cell surface [39].

Folic acid (FA) has been recently used as an effective ligand that can be bound to specific receptors on the surface of target cells to deliver therapeutic and diagnostic agents [35,41]. It is bound to folate receptor (FR) overexpressed on different types of tumor cells, including hepatocellular carcinoma [40,42,43]. Folic acid is a suitable candidate to functionalize nanoparticles because of non-immunogenic property, high stability, small molecular size, availability, high selectivity, and high binding affinity to the tumor cell surface receptors [44].

On the other side, many monoclonal antibodies (mAb) have been introduced to target vascular endothelial growth factor receptor (VEGFR) for tumor diagnosis and therapy [45]. Bevacizumab (BEV, Avastin®), a humanized mAb, is the first anti-angiogenic protein approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA, USA); it acts as an anti-human VEGF agent that is specifically bound to the VEGF receptors on the surface of cancer cells [46,47]. Bevacizumab has been used in diagnosing and treating many cancer types, such as HCC [45,48].

The present study aims to develop Gd-CDs with dual-modal FL/MR imaging functionality. Herein, non-targeted, single, and dual-targeted Gd-CDs with folic acid and/or Bevacizumab are investigated as nanoprobes to enhance targeting specificity toward hepatocellular carcinoma. Dual-targeted nanoprobes reveal high internalization in tumor regions and excellent potential for the improvement of the diagnosis of cancer by enhancing the specificity and sensitivity of their uptakes by cancer cells.

3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), antibiotic solution (penicillin-streptomycin), ethylenediamine (EDA), folic acid (FA), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), citric acid (CA), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), trypsin, Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS), and Phosphate-Buffered Saline (PBS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) was purchased from Biosera Europe, ZI du Bousquet (France). Gadobutrol (Gd–C18H31GdN4O9) was purchased from Bayer (Leverkusen, Germany). Agarose gel was obtained from Cleaver Scientific (England). Bevacizumab (Avastin) was purchased from Roche (Hoffmann-la Roche, Basel, Switzerland). Mouse hepatocellular carcinoma (Hepa1-6) and normal L929 murine fibroblast cell lines were obtained from the Pasteur Institute (Tehran, Iran).

The synthesis of Gd-CDs nanoparticles was carried out using a hydrothermal method based on the previously reported procedure [31] with some modifications. Briefly, 100 mg citric acid (as a carbon source) was dissolved in 6 mL distilled water under stirring. A few minutes later, 2 mL of Gd-DO3A-butrol and 250 μL of EDA were added to the solution. Afterward, the obtained homogeneous solution was transmitted to an autoclave for heating at 200 °C for 4 h. Next, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The excess chemicals and agglomerated large particles were removed by centrifugation (UNIVERSAL 320, Pole Ideal Tajhiz, Iran) at 12000 rpm for 15 min. Then, the supernatant was purified using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) 500–1000 Da) against deionized (DI) water overnight, and the resulting suspension was kept at 4 °C for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of single-targeted Gd-CDs with folic acid (Gd-CDs-FA)
ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid-functionalized Gd-CDs were produced by the same method for Gd-CDs formation; however, folic acid was added to the formulation. Briefly, 18 mg folic acid was added to the citric acid solution under magnetically stirring at room temperature. Folic acid is soluble in the basic media; thus, the sodium hydroxide solution was added dropwise until the suspension became clear. After adding Gd-DO3A-butrol and EDA, the suspension was heated at 200 °C for 4 h. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles and excess chemicals. Purification of the supernatant containing Gd-CDs-FA nanoprobes was performed by the dialysis membrane with MWCO of 500–1000 Da against DI water overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of single-targeted Gd-CDs with Bevacizumab (Gd-CDs-BEV)
ce:section.ce:para: Bevacizumab was activated using NHS/EDC chemistry, and the incorporated EDA amine groups on the surface of Gd-CDs were bound to the activated terminal carboxylic groups of Bevacizumab. Firstly, 4 mg of NHS and 8 mg of EDC were added to 1 mL of PBS under stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, 4 mL of Bevacizumab (50 μg mL−1) was mixed with the solution, and it was magnetically stirred for 2 h. Later, the Gd-CDs solution was added to the resulting product and incubated for 12 h under stirring at 10 °C [49]. Finally, the prepared solution was kept at 4 °C under the dark condition overnight. The final product was dialyzed by a dialysis membrane (MWCO 12–14 kDa) for 12 h to remove the free Gd-CDs and unreacted EDC and NHS.

To synthesize Gd-CDs-FA-BEV, Gd-CDs-FA was conjugated with Bevacizumab according to the method described in section 2.3.2. The resulting product was dialyzed and then stored at 4 °C. Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometer (ICP-OES) (Varian Vista-Pro, Australia) was used to estimate the gadolinium concentration within all Gd-CDs products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of functionalized and non-functionalized Gd-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The hydrodynamic (HD) size and size distribution of non-functionalized and functionalized Gd-CDs were determined using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS, Vascov/Cordouan Technologies, France). High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI TEC9G20, 200 kV, USA) was applied to determine the size and morphology characterization of non-targeted and dual-targeted Gd-CDs. Further, Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) was used to record the elemental mapping on the HR-TEM. The functional groups were also evaluated by a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (JASCO, Japan). The structure of Gd-CDs was determined by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, Germany). ICP-OES was used to measure concentrations of the Gd in Gd-CDs formulations. This analysis was performed by dissolving samples in the combined solution of hydrochloric and nitric acids, and measuring the emission of gadolinium ions by ICP-OES. The optical properties of various Gd-CDs were assessed using UV–Visible (Shimadzu UV-160 Spectrophotometer, Japan) and fluorescence (Shimadzu RF-5301PC Spectrofluorometer) spectroscopy. The fluorescence QY value of each sample was determined using a comparison of the emission intensity and absorbance value with fluorescein (QY = 95%), as a reference standard, according to the following equation [50].
(1) QYx = QYstd (Ix/Istd) (Rstd/Rx) (n2 x/n2 std)
Where I is the fluorescence intensity, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and R represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength. The subscripts std and x denote the standard (fluorescein) and test sample, respectively. Absorption of Gd-CDs and fluorescein at the excitation wavelength was kept below 0.1 by dispersing them in water (refractive index, 1.33) to minimize re-absorption effects.

For relaxivity measurements, the gadolinium concentrations in the non-targeted and targeted Gd-CDs were measured by ICP-OES. Based on previous studies [15,51], the concentration range of samples (equivalent Gd3+ concentration: 0–1 mM) was selected. T1-weighted MR imaging of Gd-CDs products and Gd-DO3A-butrol was performed by a 1.5 T MRI system (Magnetom Aera, Siemens, Germany). All Gd-CDs formulations were diluted in distilled water to create different concentrations of Gd (0, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1 mM). T1-weighted multi spin-echo (SE) imaging sequences were taken according to some scanning parameters, including echo time (TE) = 9 ms, variable repetition time (TR) values (150, 300, 500, 700, 900, 1200, 2000, and 3000 ms) [52–54], matrix size = 224 × 320, flip angle = 90°, field of view (FOV) = 192 × 220 mm2, and slice thickness = 3 mm. Image quantification analysis was performed using semi-automatic-defined region-of-interest (ROI) measurements of the signal intensity (SI) of the images by MATLAB toolbox. Signal intensities were applied as a variable to plot the T1 map, which was calculated by assuming signal decay to be mono-exponential. The TR values and calculated signal intensities were applied to compute T1 by a nonlinear function least-square curve-fitting on a voxel-by-voxel basis. To plot T1 maps, the signal intensity of voxels was calculated by the following equation [53]:
(2) SI(voxel (i,j) = S0(voxel (i,j)) [1-exp(-TR/T1(voxel (i,j)]
Where S0 was the signal intensity of water.

Longitudinal relaxation rate (R1) for each sample was expressed using equation (3):
(3) R1 = 1/T1
The longitudinal relaxivities (r1) were defined as the ratio of relaxation rates slope to Gd concentrations.

Mouse hepatocellular carcinoma (Hepa1-6) and normal L929 murine fibroblast cell lines were used in this experiment. Complete culture medium for cell growth included DMEM, 10% FBS, antibiotics mixture containing 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin, and 100 U mL−1 penicillin. The cultured cells were incubated at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere.

The cytotoxicities of non-functionalized and functionalized Gd-CDs on Hepa1-6 and L929 cells were assessed using the MTT test. The cells were cultured (2 × 104 cells per well) in 96-well plates and maintained for 24 h in the incubator with the mentioned conditions. After that, 200 μL of fresh medium containing 10% FBS with different Gd concentrations (0 (control), 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 mM) of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV was poured into each well. To uptake nanoprobes, the plates were placed in the incubator again for 24 h. After washing the cells with PBS, 100 μL of medium containing 20 μL of 0.5 mg mL−1 MTT solution was added to each well, and the cells were incubated under the dark condition for 4 h. After removing the supernatant, 100 μL of DMSO was poured into wells to dissolve the formed formazan crystals. Each experiment was repeated three times. The optical density (OD) of each sample was measured by an ELISA microplate reader (Bio-RAD 680, USA) at a wavelength of 570 nm. The percentage of cell viability was calculated in comparison to controls using the following equation:
(4) Cell viability (%) = (ODtreated group/ODcontrol group) × 100
Based on MTT results, concentrations with cell viabilities higher than 80% for all groups were used for MR imaging, fluorescence imaging, and cellular uptake analysis.

To evaluate the targeting ability of various Gd-CDs, an inverted microscope system (Nikon, Eclipse Ti–U) was used to carry out fluorescence imaging. For this purpose, Hepa1-6 (FR and BEV-receptor positive cell line) and L929 cells were cultured (2 × 105 cells per well) in a 24-well culture plate. After 24 h, cells were treated by various Gd-CDs at 0.6 mM Gd concentration for 6 h. It is noteworthy that, the lowest concentration (0.6 mM Gd) that could achieve the desired results while the cell viabilities remain higher than 80% for all groups, was chosen. Finally, the cells were washed, and the fluorescence images were acquired under UV excitation.

MR imaging of Hepa1-6 and L929 cell lines was performed using an MRI scanner with a 1.5 T magnetic field. Firstly, 3 × 106 cells of each cell line were treated by dual-targeted nanoprobe (Gd-CDs-FA-BEV) at various Gd concentrations (0 (control), 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 mM), and they were kept shaking for 4 h. After cell washing and centrifuging with PBS, they re-dispersed in PBS. Subsequently, they were suspended in 500 μL of 2% agarose solution and were prepared for imaging. The following parameters were used for T1-weighted SE imaging: TR = 500 ms, TE = 9 ms, flip angle = 90°, matrix size = 224 × 320, slice thickness = 3 mm, and FOV = 192 × 220 mm2. Finally, the signal intensities of the MR images were determined and evaluated by drawing ROI at each of the imaging samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of cellular uptake of Gd-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Gd-CDs intracellular uptake was evaluated using ICP-OES measurements. The Hepa1-6 cancer cells were cultured (1 × 106 cells per well) in 6-well plates. After 24 h, non-functionalized and functionalized Gd-CDs at 0.8 mM Gd concentration were added into the wells and incubated for 6, 12, and 24 h. Then, the cells were washed, trypsinized, and counted. After centrifuge, the cells were digested by 2 mL aqua regia. Finally, Gd3+ ions concentration in each sample was quantified using ICP-OES. The Gd3+ uptake per cell was evaluated based on Gd3+ ions concentration and cell numbers.

The analysis of obtained data was performed by SPSS software (version 15). The results, which were close to the normal distribution, were described as the mean ± SD. Each experiment was triplicated and analyzed by a one-way method. A p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The current study investigated the new dual-modal imaging probes with high QY and enhanced MRI relaxivity. For this purpose, non-targeted and single-targeted Gd-CDs with folic acid were synthesized by the single-pot hydrothermal method using CA, EDA, and Gd-DO3A-butrol. Subsequently, they were functionalized with Bevacizumab to target Hepa1-6 cancer cells via FA and BEV receptors. Therefore, Gd-CDs accumulated in cancer cells and acted as fluorescence and MR imaging nanoprobes. The diagram of the synthesis route and dual-modal application of Gd-CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of functionalized and non-functionalized Gd-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Gd-doped CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method. Citric acid, a non-toxic organic polyprotic acid with several carboxyl groups, facilitates the dehydration and carbonization. Furthermore, CA reduces the cytotoxicity of CDs [55]. In some studies, folic acid has been used as a carbon source [56] or a targeting agent [57,58] to prepare CDs or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by hydrothermal method. In both methods, FA residuals on the nanoparticle surface favorably enhance targetability to cancer cells. In this study, Gd-CDs-FA nanoprobes were synthesized using a CA and FA, in which FA residuals on the surface of Gd-CDs act as a targeting agent. It has been proved that surface-passivation reagents, especially EDA in the synthesis of CDs, would significantly improve the QY and dispersibility of the resultant CDs [59]. Targeting bioligands should be stably conjugated on nanoparticles [60]. Depending on the type and surface properties of nanoparticles, targeting agents can be bound to them by covalent or noncovalent methods [39]. In this study, Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA were conjugated covalently with Bevacizumab using well-known EDC/NHS chemistry.

Size distribution and hydrodynamic size of various Gd-CDs were determined by DLS. The HD size of the Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV were 11.7 ± 1.5, 13.6 ± 1.2, 50.9 ± 0.8, and 53.4 ± 1.3 nm, respectively. The morphology and size of the non-targeted and dual-targeted Gd-CDs were assessed using the HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), Gd-CDs have an almost spherical shape with a uniform size of 2 ± 0.5 nm. HR-TEM image of Gd-CDs-FA-BEV (Fig. 1(b)) illustrates dendritic structures with a mean diameter of 40 nm, probably due to the accumulation of Gd-CDs (darker dots in the image) in the protein structure of Bevacizumab. HD diameter of Gd-CDs in DLS was considerably larger than the dry-state diameter in TEM images. The HD size of Gd-CDs-FA was slightly larger than the Gd-CDs due to the small size of folic acid. However, the HD size of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA increased significantly after conjugation with large BEV molecules. The EDS test confirmed the presence of the Gd element within Gd-CDs-FA-BEV (Fig. 1(c)).

According to Fig. 1(d), the XRD pattern of Gd-CDs includes a main large and wide-diffraction peak centered around 2Ɵ = 12.4°, probably attributed to intercalated graphite oxide or graphene oxide (GO) [33], and three minor peaks with a high full width half maximum (FWHM) and low altitude at 22.9°, 28.5°, and 34.6°. The broad scattering background, the decrease in the intensities of the three peaks, and the increase in FWHM evidence the amorphous nature of synthesized Gd-CDs [33], which is in line with the previously reported results [61]. The XRD pattern does not demonstrate the existence of Gd‏ in the Gd‏-doped CDs (Fig. 1(d)). According to the report released by Wu et al. [62], metals doping in carbon dots show no obvious change in the XRD pattern.

The surface of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV is also characterized using FTIR analysis. As shown in Fig. 1(e), the asymmetric stretching vibrations at 1185 cm−1 and bending vibrations at 1328 cm−1 are ascribed to C–N and CN of the protein ring. The absorption peak around 3400 cm−1 is equivalent to O–H and N–H stretching vibration, whereas those at 1592 cm−1 and 1394 cm−1 are attributed to CO stretching vibrations and C–O bending vibration, respectively. The characteristic IR absorption peaks of folic acid due to N–H bending vibration of the CONH group and CO amide stretching, are apparent in the spectrum [56]. Furthermore, the spectrum of Gd-CDs-FA-BEV shows several characteristic peaks at 2861 cm−1 (−CH stretch),‏ a broad peak at around 1600 cm−l (Amide I and Amide II), and many peaks within the range of 500–1300 cm−1 [63].

The optical characteristics of all formulations were evaluated by UV–Visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Their UV–Vis spectra show a broad absorption band with two maximum absorbance peaks at 240 nm and 348 nm (Fig. 2 (a)). The absorption peak at 240 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC band [31]. Another peak at 348 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition of CO band [31]. As seen in Fig. 2(a) (inset), all formulations are transparent solutions under natural light and emit blue fluorescence under UV light.

Fig. 2(b) illustrates the fluorescence intensities of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV at the 360 nm excitation wavelength. Besides, the fluorescence intensities of Gd-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (320–400 nm) (Fig. 2(c)) exhibit an excitation-dependent emission behavior common for fluorescent CDs [64]. The QYs of the Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV were calculated as 49.94%, 61.22%, 82.5%, and 83.67%, respectively, which are greater than other reported CDs prepared through hydrothermal method [33,55]. Although the enhancement mechanisms of Gd-CDs quantum yields are not clear, they can be attributed to disorders in the carbon rings induced by the doped Gd ions [65,66]. Also, adding the surface-passivation reagents such as EDA during the synthesis can significantly increase the QY of the resultant CDs [59,67]. On the other side, a significant enhancement of QY is observed after the addition of FA. The presence of FA on the Gd-CDs surface locates amine groups on the surface and could change their optical behavior and enhance their luminescence efficiency [56,57,67].

To investigate the potential application of synthesized nanoprobes as new MRI contrast agents, their magnetic relaxation properties were evaluated compared to the clinical Gd-based contrast agent (Gd-DO3A-butrol). To achieve this goal, samples were diluted in distilled water on the same concentrations of Gd, and T1 images were taken (Fig. 3 (a)). As observed in Fig. 3(b), R1 values of various Gd-CDs were improved linearly with the increased Gd concentration, demonstrating the Gd concentration-dependent contrast enhancement. Overall, the potential of the MRI contrast agents was evaluated by its relaxivity (r1), which is recognized as the slope of the plot of R1 versus Gd concentration. The relaxivities (r1) of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA, Gd-CDs-BEV, and Gd-CDs-FA-BEV were calculated 7.98, 7.83, 7.61 and 6.03 mM−1 s−1, respectively, which are higher than that for Gd-DO3A-butrol (r1 = 4.01 mM−1 s−1). It should be noted that small size is essential for nanoparticles to yield high relaxivity since r1 depends on the interactions of hydrogen protons with Gd3+ ions. The Gd-CDs with small sizes have a high special surface ratio, increasing interactions of hydrogen protons with Gd3+ ions. Therefore, r1 increases for these nanoparticles [6,31,68,69]. Furthermore, relaxivities (r1) of various Gd-CDs were comparable with other Gd-CDs reported previously [33,59,70]. The results confirmed that various synthesized Gd-CDs could be used as MRI contrast agents for bioimaging and improving the relaxivity of the clinical products.

Cytotoxicity is one of the main concerns for bioimaging probes applications [51,71]. Therefore, the toxicity effect of various Gd-CDs on cancer and normal cells was evaluated by MTT assay before cell imaging. The Hepa1-6 and L929 cells were incubated with non-functionalized and functionalized Gd-CDs at various Gd concentrations for 24 h. Fig. 4 shows that after 24 h incubation, all nanoprobes exerted no significant impact on the viability of both cancer and normal cell lines, and cell viability rate decreased with a Gd concentration-dependent trend for all groups. The viabilities percent of Hepa1-6 cells was higher than 80% within the concentration range used. Additionally, targeted Gd-CDs exhibited a superior cytotoxic effect compared to Gd-CDs alone for Hepa1-6 cells. Moreover, dual-targeted Gd-CDs showed higher cytotoxicity compared to single-targeted Gd-CDs. This could be correlated to the enhancement of nanoparticle uptake and internalization into the Heapa1-6 cells since both BEV and FA receptors are overexpressed on the surface of these cells. Furthermore, the viability rate of treated cancer cells with Gd-CDs-BEV was lower than Gd-CDs-FA groups, possibly due to the therapeutic properties of the Bevacizumab antibody as a chemotherapy drug. The MTT results showed that for all formulations and concentrations, the viability of L929 cells was above 90%, indicating the non-toxicity and biocompatibility of synthesized nanoprobes for normal cells. The results confirmed the slight toxic effect and good biological compatibility of the various prepared Gd-CDs in bioimaging applications.

The potential application of the synthesized Gd-CDs for cell labeling and fluorescence imaging was assessed by incubating Hepa1-6 and L929 cells with non-functionalized and functionalized Gd-CDs (0.6 mM Gd concentration) for 6 h, and fluorescence microscopic images were captured under excitation of UV light and bright-field. The Hepa1-6 cells exhibited bright blue colors under UV excitation (Fig. 5 (a)), correlated with emission spectra of various Gd-CDs. As can be seen visually, targeted Gd-CDs have a higher uptake in cancer cells compared to non-targeted Gd-CDs, probably due to specific internalization of nanoprobes via folate/VEGF receptors. More importantly, there was a higher level of dual-targeted Gd-CDs uptake compared with single-targeted nanoprobes in cancer cells. On the contrary, as shown in Fig. 5(b), no fluorescence was observed for the normal cells, indicating a strong binding affinity of FA and BEV as cancer biomarkers. The bright-field images revealed that both cell lines treated with various Gd-CDs maintained their living morphology during the imaging test, further verifying the biocompatibility of Gd-CDs. These findings successfully demonstrated that the Gd-CDs nanoprobes, synthesized via a nontoxic CA, had good biocompatibility with a high level of specific cell internalization, as promising nanoprobes for fluorescence imaging.

It is noteworthy that, for in-vivo imaging applications, fluorophores with long emission at the near-infrared (NIR) region are generally preferred to overcome the photon attenuation in living tissue. The maximum emission of Gd-CDs synthesized in this study locates at the blue light region, which limits their in-vivo application due to the low penetration in a biological system. However, MR imaging provides excellent tissue penetration, high spatial resolution, and soft tissue contrast, which can overcome the limitations of fluorescence imaging and complement the defects of this emission light. Therefore, Gd-CDs with blue light emission can be used for in-vitro fluorescence imaging tissues and fixed cells.

To further confirm the cancer cell-specific targeting ability and MR imaging performance of dual-targeted Gd-CDs, both Hepa1-6 and L929 cells were incubated with various concentrations of Gd-CDs-FA-BEV for 6 h. The cell samples were scanned by the previously described MRI system (Fig. 6 (a)). T1-weighted MR images showed a significant enhancement of signal intensity in treated Hepa1-6 cells compared to the control cells (Fig. 6(b)). Moreover, the signal intensity improved with increasing Gd concentration. In contrast, under the same condition, no significant difference was observed between MRI signals in L929 cells incubated with different concentrations of Gd-CDs-FA-BEV. Therefore, the signal intensity of these cells was slightly higher than that of the control group. Furthermore, Hepa1-6 cells exhibited a higher MRI signal than L929 cells in all concentrations of nanoprobe (p-value <0.05). The significant difference in MR signal intensity between two cell lines could be attributed to the specific cellular uptake of the Gd-CDs-FA-BEV by Hepa1-6 cells due to overexpressing BEV and folate receptors compared with L929 cells. These results demonstrated that dual-targeted Gd-CDs, prepared in the current study, could be efficiently used as a T1-weighted MRI contrast agent for molecular cancer imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of Gd-CDs cellular uptake by ICP-OES
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular internalization of all Gd-CDs formulations in Hepa1-6 cells was quantified by ICP-OES (Fig. 6(c)). Cellular uptake of Gd3+ ions by cancer cells for all samples was increased by incubation time. However, functionalized Gd-CDs exhibited higher cellular uptake compared to non-functionalized Gd-CDs. Further, dual-targeted Gd-CDs had the highest level of intracellular uptake so that their internalization in Hepa1-6 cells after 24 h incubation was 5-fold much as that by the non-functionalized Gd-CDs. ICP-OES results further proved the key role of the conjugation of Gd-CDs with folic acid and Bevacizumab in enhancing cellular uptake efficiency.

In summary, non-targeted and targeted Gd-CDs, as dual-modal nanoprobes for FL/MR imaging, were synthesized by the hydrothermal method in the present research. Several reliable and precise characterization examinations proved that synthesized Gd-CDs had nano-size, excellent photoluminescence properties, such as high fluorescence QY, and significantly stronger MRI response with higher r1 value than the clinical MRI contrast agent (Gd-DO3A-butrol). MTT assay showed that Gd-CDs had a low toxicity. Additionally, cross-validation between the fluorescence and MR imaging results supported the idea that synthesized nanoprobes had efficient cell labeling and cell-membrane permeability, being successfully internalized into cancer cells compared to normal cells. These features make Gd-CDs up-and-coming candidates for biomedical applications as dual-modal FL/MR imaging nanoprobes, providing complementary information, e.g., high resolution and sensitivity and deep tissue penetration. Furthermore, dual-active targeting of Gd-CDs using folic acid and Bevacizumab resulted in more internalization into Hepa1-6 cells compared to single-targeted and non-targeted Gd-CDs. Consequently, the current research results suggest that novel nano-sized dual-targeted Gd-CDs can significantly improve diagnostic specificity and sensitivity of imaging and provide a more accurate and reliable cancer diagnosis.
